behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources.
behaviors.justification: This tests orchestration logic — whether the data-collection layer correctly composes results from three sources before passing to the summary generator. A unit test with three mocked sources can verify that all three results are present in the composed data. Mocking cannot hide failure here: if the code forgets to fetch one source or doesn't include its result, the mock will expose it.
behaviors.test_description: Mock all three data sources. Verify that the data structure passed to the summary generator contains fields/keys from all three sources. Verify that fetching results from each source is called exactly once.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a `data unavailable` note for that section rather than aborting.
behaviors.justification: While the core orchestration logic (catch exception, don't abort, include note) could be tested at Unit level with a simple mock exception, real failures depend on realistic error modes. The code might have timeout handling, HTTP client retry logic, or exception type discrimination that Unit mocking will not exercise correctly. For example, a mocked immediate exception differs from a 30-second timeout; different HTTP error codes might require different handling; connection-refused might behave differently than a 500 response. Integration testing with realistic failure conditions (actual timeouts, connection errors, specific HTTP status codes) ensures the resilience behavior works in practice.
behaviors.test_description: Set up two parallel test scenarios: (a) one data source returns HTTP 500 or raises a realistic connection error (via a test HTTP server or realistic mock), others return valid data; (b) one source times out (using a real timeout, or time-mocked delay), others return valid data. In each case, verify that the report is generated with a 'data unavailable' note and does not abort.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit-level test with a mocked generic exception would miss bugs in timeout handling, retry logic, or exception-type-specific branching. Integration level with realistic failure modes is needed to catch those real failures.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data.
behaviors.justification: If summary generation is deterministic code (template engine, rule-based extraction), Unit level is sufficient — mock input data and verify the output structure. However, if (as is common in modern systems) the summary is generated by an LLM, only a real LLM call can verify that the summary accurately represents the input. Mocking an LLM to return a canned response will always pass; real failures (LLM confabulation, hallucination, ignoring key values) are invisible to mocks. The behavior specifically requires *accurate representation*, which depends on the model's actual output.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a standardized test dataset with known key values (e.g., total issues = 42, deployments = 5, error rate = 2.3%). Call the summary generation with a real LLM (not a mock). Parse or inspect the summary text and verify that it explicitly mentions or correctly reflects each key value. Test both with typical data and with edge cases (zero values, very large numbers, special characters).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Assumes summary generation involves an LLM call; if it is purely deterministic code, downgrade to Unit level. The justification rests on real LLM output being non-deterministic and required to validate correctness.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly.
behaviors.justification: This is end-to-end verification that an email reaches a real mailbox. Mocking the email service will always succeed; only real email delivery (to a real SMTP server, or at minimum a test mail server with inbox inspection) can verify the report actually arrives. The failure boundary crosses process and network boundaries: the email client library must correctly format the message, the SMTP server must accept it, and it must land in the recipient's inbox. These cannot be tested with in-process mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test email service (real SMTP server, or test mail service like MailHog). Run the complete report generation and delivery. Verify that an email arrives in the configured recipient's test inbox with correct subject, recipient, and report content. Verify the email is readable and formatted correctly.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run.
behaviors.justification: This behavior involves file system I/O and fallback/retry logic. A pure in-process mock of the file system will not catch real bugs in file write operations, file permissions, path handling, or file format correctness. Similarly, a mocked email service won't reveal issues with the retry logic's state management or cleanup. Integration testing with real file system I/O and realistic email service failure (connection refused, timeout) ensures the fallback actually works. The second part (retry on next run) can be tested by simulating two execution cycles without requiring a real scheduler.
behaviors.test_description: First execution: make the email service unreachable (mock it to fail, or point to a non-existent endpoint). Verify that the report is saved to a local file with correct content and location. Verify the file is readable and valid (check format, permissions, size). Second execution: make the email service available again, trigger the retry logic, verify the report is read from the file and sent successfully, and verify the file is cleaned up or marked as sent.
